title: From Disk to CPU: How Linux Runs Your Programs
topic: Understand ELF files and how Linux executes programs.
summary: A first-principles, analogy-rich tour of ELF files and program execution on Linux — following one tiny C program from source through execve, the kernel's segment loader, the dynamic linker, and into main(), with hands-on readelf/strace callouts throughout.

concepts.title: The ELF Header: The File's ID Card
Segments: How the Kernel Builds Your Process
The Dynamic Linker and Shared Libraries
First Instructions: From Kernel to Your main()

images.prompt: A wide technical blueprint-style illustration on a deep navy ground with a faint cyan grid: on the left, a stylized disk platter with a small file icon labeled with a terminal-style "a.out" glyph (no readable body text); a dotted cyan trace runs rightward through a schematic cross-section of a layered file (a thin header bar, a few stacked rectangular "segment" blocks) and continues into a glowing CPU chip outline on the right, like a circuit trace connecting storage to silicon. Clean vector line-art, engineering-schematic aesthetic, cyan (#46b1e0) and soft teal (#7fd1c8) linework on navy (#0c2233), amber (#f0a15a) accent highlight at the CPU, generous whitespace, no baked-in body text. Target size ~1280x720px, 16:9 landscape; compose for a hero banner with the subject centered and safe margins — it is letterboxed into a 16:9 box, so keep nothing important near the edges.
images.alt: A technical blueprint illustration of a program's journey from a disk platter through a stylized ELF file schematic into a CPU chip
images.caption: The trace from platter to processor — a program's journey, drawn as a circuit.
